entryTemplate.title: Short customer-facing title
entryTemplate.summary: A clear paragraph: what changed and why readers should care.
entryTemplate.detail: Optional. Extra paragraphs separated by blank lines (\n\n). No commits or ticket IDs.

entries.title: Policy Tracker: news lane goes live and the page now reads from two registry layers
entries.summary: The Policy Tracker's "Live news lane" is now actually live. Headlines from the news feed (Dawn, The News, Business Recorder, Reuters, FT, Bloomberg, and 20+ more outlets) are matched in real time to each tracked policy, so when something moves on a rule we're following you see the coverage on the same page as the rule itself.
entries.detail:
When we launched the Policy Tracker earlier today, the news lane was a placeholder — each policy showed which keywords we'd be watching for. That placeholder is gone. The lane now pulls live from the same news feed that powers the dashboard, runs each policy's keyword set against every recent headline, and surfaces the most recent matches next to the policy they touch. Click through goes straight to the original outlet. We don't summarize, paraphrase, or rerank by tone.

Underneath the page, the registry now reads from two layers instead of one. The first is the editor-curated fixture you already saw: eight cited records across the eight domains, written by us. The second is an automated layer produced by our ingestion pipeline. Today the automated layer contributes mostly transparency (which official sources our pipeline reached, what it found) — it doesn't add records to the registry yet. As we wire each adapter and stand up the structured-extraction step, those records will start flowing into the auto layer. The editor-curated layer always wins on conflict, so the records you trust today will not get overwritten by anything machine-derived. Records the pipeline does contribute will carry a small "Auto-ingested" badge so you can always tell what came from where.

A daily scheduled run now refreshes the auto layer at 08:00 PKT. Anything the pipeline cannot reach is logged in the file itself; no broken sources hide silently.

For what's still ahead: the IMF press-release feed needs rediscovery (their RSS endpoint was retired and we won't ship brittle scraping), the State Bank's monetary-policy index needs a stable source endpoint, and the structured-extraction step for less-structured documents (FBR notifications, NEPRA tariffs, SECP circulars) requires a separate decision on the LLM provider we'll use. Those land in subsequent Phase 2 releases without disrupting anything you see today.

entries.title: Pakistan Policy Tracker: every government rule that matters, in one place
entries.summary: We added a live registry of Pakistan's policy moves across eight domains—fiscal, monetary, trade, energy, regulatory, social, foreign, and security. Each entry is sourced, dated, and traceable back to an original SBP statement, FBR notification, NEPRA determination, IMF release, or World Bank indicator. The page sits inside the signed-in research hub alongside GDP History.
entries.detail:
Pakistan's policy environment moves fast and across many regulators. Following the State Bank's monetary stance, NEPRA tariff decisions, FBR notifications, the latest IMF review, and BISP coverage usually means watching half a dozen websites and trying to remember what each agency last announced. The Policy Tracker collects those moves into one filterable view so you can read across them rather than chasing each in isolation.

Three audience presets shape the page differently. Investors and operators see fiscal, monetary, and trade policy first, with currency and energy decisions surfaced as recent moves. Researchers and journalists get a registry-first view with sourcing and dates emphasized. Policy professionals see the full domain matrix with status flags for live, draft, pending, and amended rules.

Every record links back to the original document we built the entry from—an SBP statement, an FBR SRO, a NEPRA determination, an IMF press release, or a World Bank indicator series. We refuse to cite anything we cannot point you to. A methodology panel on the page explains how we pick what to track and how we summarize.

This is a Phase 1 release with eight curated records seeded across seven of the eight domains; trade will populate as current actions are announced. A more automated ingestion pipeline is in flight and will gradually widen coverage without changing the page experience for readers.

entries.title: Sign-in restored and protected by a new automated check
entries.summary: Sign-in is working again on every page. A recent layout change accidentally removed a tiny element the sign-in page expected to find, which silently stopped the Google button and the magic-link email form from responding. Accounts were never at risk—the page just couldn’t wire itself up.
entries.detail:
We patched the page to tolerate the missing element so the same shape of bug cannot block sign-in again.

Beyond the patch, we added a dedicated automated check to the status pipeline that opens a real incident the moment any required element on the sign-in (or paid-access) page goes missing, or any inline script tries to use an element that is not in the page. That means the public status page will now reflect this class of regression instead of staying green while users are stuck.

Thank you to everyone who noticed and reported it.

entries.title: Release notes: major milestones and fuller explanations
entries.summary: The public release-notes page now opens with major product chapters spelled out in depth. Weekly updates still appear below, but each item shows the full story—what changed, why it matters, and how you might notice it when using brief.pk.
entries.detail:
You should not need an engineering background to understand this page. “Major milestones” are the big arcs of the product (for example the core dashboard, GDP History, and intelligence search). Everything else is grouped by week, and every note is written so you can read it start to finish.

We still do not show raw commit history or internal ticket numbers here—the goal is trust and clarity for readers, not a developer log.

entries.title: One consistent footer across the public site and status pages
entries.summary: Legal links, brand context, research shortcuts, and the aggregated-intelligence credit line now follow the same layout everywhere. If you are looking for Privacy, Terms, GDP History, or how to reach the team, the footer behaves predictably no matter which page you landed on.
entries.detail: Small inconsistencies—different footers on different templates—make a product feel unmaintained even when the underlying data is solid. Aligning footers reduces that friction and makes compliance pages easier to find under stress.

entries.title: Less sensitive operational detail on public status surfaces
entries.summary: We reviewed what the public status site should reveal about internal routing and providers. Anything that was helpful only to operators—not to readers—was removed or generalized so the surface stays trustworthy without becoming a map of our stack.
entries.detail: Security improvements are not always visible features. This change is about reducing accidental disclosure while keeping honest health reporting. If you rely on the status page, you should still see whether core endpoints respond—but not internal configuration trivia.

entries.title: Status site custom domain
entries.summary: Traffic for the health dashboard is served from our dedicated status domain so bookmarks, incident posts, and support emails can point to one stable URL.
entries.detail: Custom domains on GitHub Pages require careful DNS setup. Once in place, readers see a URL that matches the rest of the brief.pk ecosystem.

entries.title: Clearer status wording for sign-in paths
entries.summary: Login health rows on the status site use plainer language so you can tell whether magic link, Google, and the login page themselves are reachable—without jargon about implementation.
entries.detail: ‘Reachable’ versus ‘configured’ can confuse people during an incident. Copy now matches how readers actually think about logging in.

entries.title: Login paths tracked alongside core APIs
entries.summary: Magic link, Google OAuth, and the login page itself appear next to news, markets, and other components so you can see account access health in the same glance as content feeds.
entries.detail: Users blame ‘the site’ when sign-in fails—even if articles still load. Elevating login checks makes incidents easier to diagnose and communicate.

entries.title: More resilient login health during partial rollouts
entries.summary: When health payloads are mid-deploy, the status UI now degrades gracefully instead of flashing alarming states that do not match what end users experience.
entries.detail: Synthetic checks sometimes race ahead of production config. Fallbacks keep the dashboard honest: show uncertainty explicitly rather than guessing red.

entries.title: Mobile layout: easier reading on phones
entries.summary: Spacing, safe areas, scrollable tables, and card wrapping were tuned so the dashboard remains usable on small screens—especially when markets move and you are not at a desk.
entries.detail: Pakistan-heavy audiences often reach for a phone first. These refinements reduce pinch-zooming and sideways scrolling on common breakpoints without changing the underlying data.

entries.title: Safer rendering when payloads are incomplete
entries.summary: If a probe returns partial JSON, panels skip missing fields instead of blanking the whole page—especially important during deploy windows.
entries.detail: Defensive coding here protects readers from transient infrastructure hiccups that are not ‘outages’ in the user-facing sense.

entries.title: Status dashboard redesign
entries.summary: The public status page now mirrors how engineers think about uptime—component grid, incident banner, and per-service bars—but stays readable for non-technical stakeholders.
entries.detail: The goal was transparency without noise. Colour, typography, and spacing follow the same brand system as the main product so the two sites feel related.

entries.title: Search endpoint in monitoring docs
entries.summary: Runbooks and status expectations now treat merged search the same as core news: one more step toward consistent on-call coverage.
entries.detail: Documentation drifts fast on small teams. This ties observability words to what actually ships in production.

entries.title: Lighter visual theme for the status site
entries.summary: Higher contrast hierarchy and calmer backgrounds make long incident days easier on the eyes—especially on laptop screens.
entries.detail: Pure aesthetics sometimes support comprehension: when hierarchy is obvious, people parse severity faster.

entries.title: Scheduled health snapshots (ongoing)
entries.summary: Automation continues to commit fresh status snapshots on a regular cadence so charts and JSON history stay current without manual babysitting.
entries.detail: Readers rarely notice this work directly, but it keeps timelines honest when you scroll backward through incidents.

entries.title: Deeper synthetic analytics in status tooling
entries.summary: Operator-facing panels gained journey-style signals and richer component analytics so we can see behaviour across probes—not just one green/red boolean per route.
entries.detail: This primarily helps the team maintain reliability; readers still see the same high-level health story, with more accuracy behind the scenes.

entries.title: Public status site on GitHub Pages
entries.summary: We published an always-on status dashboard with automated checks, historical JSON artifacts, and a layout aligned with brief.pk branding.
entries.detail: External status pages build trust: when news or markets hiccup, a neutral health surface reduces rumor and duplicate reports.

entries.title: Intelligence Search and a richer Pulse experience
entries.summary: Header search now treats the whole Pakistan news corpus as one intelligence surface: the same merged feeds that power the main dashboard, plus fresh results when you need them, sorted with newest stories first. Pulse continues to explain “what matters now” in plain language for readers who want context without digging through dozens of headlines.
entries.detail:
If you follow Pakistan closely, you often care about both speed (“what just broke?”) and recall (“what did we already know?”). Intelligence Search is designed for that dual need. When you type a query, results draw from the same article pool as the live news experience so your search, Pulse, and the main feed stay aligned instead of contradicting each other.

Results you see can come from the core merged feed or from a short-lived supplemental lookup when the topic needs fresher headlines. The interface shows where each result came from so you can judge how to use it. We also added habits power users expect: recent queries, chips that relate to what Pulse is watching, shareable URLs with your search text, and keyboard shortcuts so you can move quickly without hunting for tiny controls.

Pulse sits alongside search on purpose—it is the “executive summary” layer. It aims to answer what is happening, what may unfold soon, and how confident the read is, without asking you to interpret raw model output. Together, search plus Pulse make brief.pk feel closer to a single workspace than a static headline page.

entries.title: Pakistan GDP History: decades of context in one research-grade view
entries.summary: We shipped a dedicated GDP History experience that stretches from independence through today, layers in multilateral outlooks where appropriate, and places economic turning points next to governance and crisis context so you can see why the curve moved—not just the numbers.
entries.detail:
Macro statistics on their own are easy to misread. A GDP print or revision can look small on a chart but mean a great deal for debt, inflation, or reform timing. The GDP History module was built for readers who want the long arc: growth episodes, sharp contractions, recoveries, and how Pakistan compares to its own past.

The view combines long-run historical estimates with more recent official and multilateral series, and pairs the chart with narrative and markers so a student, journalist, or diaspora investor can understand what changed in each era. The intent is not to replace formal national accounts work—it is to give credible, well-sourced context in one place.

If you only use brief.pk for news and markets, think of GDP History as the “slow journalism” companion: fewer headlines, more framing, and numbers you can cite with confidence because the sources are documented on the page.

entries.title: The Pakistan Intelligence Dashboard: news, markets, map, and research in one hub
entries.summary: brief.pk launched as a single authenticated workspace for Pakistan-focused intelligence—curated news, market snapshot (including USD/PKR and PSX context), city-level map telemetry, macro and security-economy research modules, and admin-backed controls so the product can evolve without fragile hard-coding.
entries.detail:
Most readers bounce between a news app, a broker terminal, a weather or AQI site, and scattered PDFs for macro data. The core dashboard was an explicit bet that Pakistan watchers need one reliable home screen: fast headlines when something is moving, market context when FX or equities lead the story, and research panels when the narrative is really about inflation, reserves, reform, or stability.

Auth (magic link and Google) keeps the experience tailored while protecting heavier features. The admin console lets operators enable or pause sections responsibly as sources and costs change. That foundation is what later made Intelligence Search, GDP History, and deeper AI interpretation possible without splintering into unrelated micro-sites.

If you are new to brief.pk, start with the main dashboard: live news, Pulse when it is enabled for your account, markets strip, and the map. Everything else is organized as modules so you can go deep when you need to—not before.
